---
title: "Ch 5 — Quadrilaterals"
subtitle: "Parallelograms, rectangles, rhombi, squares, trapezoids — a hierarchy of properties"
lesson-id: "kg1.quadrilaterals"
tier: "kg1"
kiselev-book: "Geometry, Book I (Planimetry)"
kiselev-chapter: 5
kiselev-chapter-title: "Parallelograms and trapezoids"
kiselev-sections: "Planimetry §§97–115 (parallelograms, special parallelograms, trapezoids)"
prerequisites: [kg1.parallels, kg1.triangle-congruence]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [game-dev, research]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state and use the *alternate-interior-angles* theorem
   from [`kg1.parallels`](../04-parallels/index.qmd)?
2. Are SSS, SAS, ASA congruence reflexes for you? See
   [`kg1.triangle-congruence`](../02-triangle-congruence/index.qmd).
3. Do you accept that the angles of any triangle sum to $180°$?
4. Can you split a four-sided figure into two triangles by drawing
   one diagonal?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

Four sticks hinged at the corners. Drag one corner — watch the
shape deform. Most positions give *some* quadrilateral; a few
special ones give the famous named figures.

```{ojs}
viewof shape = Inputs.radio(
  ["parallelogram", "rectangle", "rhombus", "square", "trapezoid", "general quadrilateral"],
  {value: "parallelogram", label: "Quadrilateral type:"}
)
```

```{ojs}
viewof a = Inputs.range([1, 5], {value: 4, step: 0.1, label: "Side length a (or width)"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof b = Inputs.range([1, 5], {value: 2.5, step: 0.1, label: "Side length b (or height)"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof angle = Inputs.range([20, 160], {value: 70, step: 1, label: "Tilt angle (parallelogram/rhombus)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 460, h = 320, padX = 40, padY = 40;
  const xS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-1, 7]).range([padX, w - padX]);
  const yS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-1, 6]).range([h - padY, padY]);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");

  const rad = (deg) => deg * Math.PI / 180;
  let pts;
  if (shape === "rectangle") {
    pts = [[0,0],[a,0],[a,b],[0,b]];
  } else if (shape === "square") {
    pts = [[0,0],[a,0],[a,a],[0,a]];
  } else if (shape === "rhombus") {
    const ax = a, ay = 0;
    const bx = a * Math.cos(rad(angle));
    const by = a * Math.sin(rad(angle));
    pts = [[0,0],[ax,ay],[ax + bx, ay + by],[bx, by]];
  } else if (shape === "parallelogram") {
    const ax = a, ay = 0;
    const bx = b * Math.cos(rad(angle));
    const by = b * Math.sin(rad(angle));
    pts = [[0,0],[ax,ay],[ax + bx, ay + by],[bx, by]];
  } else if (shape === "trapezoid") {
    pts = [[0,0],[a,0],[a - 0.7, b],[0.5, b]];
  } else {
    pts = [[0,0],[a,0],[a - 0.4, b],[0.6, b - 0.6]];
  }
  const labels = ["A","B","C","D"];

  // diagonals
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(pts[0][0])).attr("y1", yS(pts[0][1]))
    .attr("x2", xS(pts[2][0])).attr("y2", yS(pts[2][1]))
    .attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-dasharray", "3,2");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(pts[1][0])).attr("y1", yS(pts[1][1]))
    .attr("x2", xS(pts[3][0])).attr("y2", yS(pts[3][1]))
    .attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-dasharray", "3,2");

  // polygon
  svg.append("polygon")
    .attr("points", pts.map(p => `${xS(p[0])},${yS(p[1])}`).join(" "))
    .attr("fill", "#26a").attr("fill-opacity", 0.18)
    .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 2);

  // labels
  pts.forEach((p, i) => {
    svg.append("text").attr("x", xS(p[0]) + 6).attr("y", yS(p[1]) - 4)
      .attr("font-size", 12).attr("font-weight", "bold").text(labels[i]);
  });

  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY - 10)
    .attr("font-size", 12).text(`shape: ${shape}`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

A *general* quadrilateral has four sides and four angles — period.
The named ones add **constraints** (parallel sides, right angles,
equal sides). The hierarchy: **parallelogram** (both opposite sides
parallel) → **rectangle** (parallelogram + right angle) →
**square** (rectangle + equal sides). **Rhombus** = parallelogram +
equal sides. **Trapezoid** = exactly one pair of parallel sides.

## Wonder

1. A parallelogram has both pairs of opposite sides parallel. Must
   the *opposite sides* be equal in length? Why?
2. A rectangle has four right angles. Could you have *three* right
   angles and the fourth different? Try to draw one.
3. A rhombus has four equal sides. Are its diagonals related to
   the sides — perpendicular, equal, bisecting?
4. A trapezoid has exactly one pair of parallel sides. The two
   *non-parallel* sides — when do they have equal length, making the
   trapezoid *isosceles*?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) Yes — opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal (proved
below). (2) Three right angles forces $360° - 270° = 90°$ for the
fourth, so all four are right — every "$3$-right-angled
quadrilateral" is a rectangle. (3) The diagonals of a rhombus
**bisect each other and are perpendicular** (and they bisect the
angles at the vertices too). (4) An *isosceles trapezoid* has
equal non-parallel sides and equal base-angles.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal

Let $ABCD$ be a parallelogram with $AB \parallel CD$ and $AD
\parallel BC$. Show $|AB| = |CD|$ and $|AD| = |BC|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Draw diagonal $AC$. Compare $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle CDA$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Diagonal $AC$. In $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle CDA$:
$|AC| = |CA|$ shared; $\angle BAC = \angle DCA$ (alternate-interior
angles for $AB \parallel CD$ with transversal $AC$); $\angle BCA =
\angle DAC$ (alternate-interior for $AD \parallel BC$ with same
transversal). By **ASA**, $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA$.
Hence $|AB| = |CD|$ and $|BC| = |AD|$. **QED.** *(Planimetry §99.)*
</details>

### Problem 2 — Diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other

In parallelogram $ABCD$, the diagonals $AC$ and $BD$ meet at $O$.
Show $|AO| = |OC|$ and $|BO| = |OD|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle AOB$ and $\triangle COD$ — use ASA with the
alternate-interior angles.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle AOB$ and $\triangle COD$: $|AB| = |CD|$ (Problem 1);
$\angle OAB = \angle OCD$ (alternate-interior for $AB \parallel
CD$); $\angle OBA = \angle ODC$ (same reason). By **ASA**,
congruent. So $|AO| = |OC|$ and $|BO| = |OD|$. **QED.**
*(Planimetry §100.)*
</details>

### Problem 3 — Converse: bisecting diagonals ⇒ parallelogram

Suppose $ABCD$ is a quadrilateral whose diagonals bisect each other
at $O$. Show $ABCD$ is a parallelogram.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle AOB$ and $\triangle COD$ are congruent by SAS using the
vertical angle at $O$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|AO| = |OC|$, $|BO| = |OD|$ (given), $\angle AOB = \angle COD$
(vertical). By **SAS**, $\triangle AOB \cong \triangle COD$. Hence
$|AB| = |CD|$ and $\angle OAB = \angle OCD$ — these are alternate-
interior angles for transversal $AC$ cutting lines $AB$ and $CD$,
which are therefore parallel. Similarly $AD \parallel BC$. **A
parallelogram.** *(Planimetry §101.)*
</details>

### Problem 4 — Rectangle ⇔ parallelogram with one right angle

Show that a parallelogram with **one** right angle is a rectangle
(all four angles right).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Opposite angles in a parallelogram are equal (corollary of Problem
1; or directly from alternate-interior angles). Adjacent angles
sum to $180°$ (co-interior). So one $90°$ angle forces the others:
the opposite is $90°$, and each adjacent is $180° - 90° = 90°$.
**All four are $90°$.** *(Planimetry §103.)*
</details>

### Problem 5 — Rhombus diagonals are perpendicular bisectors

In a rhombus $ABCD$ (parallelogram with all sides equal), the
diagonals $AC$ and $BD$ meet at $O$. Show $AC \perp BD$ and that
each diagonal bisects the angles at its endpoints.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle AOB \cong \triangle COB$ via SSS — what does that say
about the angles at $O$ and at $B$?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By Problem 2, $|AO| = |OC|$ and $|BO| = |OD|$. In $\triangle AOB$
and $\triangle COB$: $|AB| = |CB|$ (rhombus sides equal), $|AO| =
|OC|$ (above), $|OB|$ shared. By **SSS**, congruent. Hence
$\angle AOB = \angle COB$. They are linear-pair adjacent (along
diagonal $AC$), so each is $90°$ — i.e. $BD \perp AC$.
Also $\angle ABO = \angle CBO$, so $BD$ bisects $\angle ABC$.
Same argument for $AC$ at the other vertices. **QED.**
*(Planimetry §107.)*
</details>

### Problem 6 — The midsegment of a trapezoid

Let $ABCD$ be a trapezoid with $AB \parallel CD$ (the *parallel
sides*; $|AB| = a$ and $|CD| = b$). Let $M, N$ be the midpoints of
$AD$ and $BC$ respectively. Show that $MN \parallel AB$ and
$|MN| = \dfrac{a + b}{2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Extend $DM$ to meet line $BC$ at $E$. Or: cut off a parallelogram
of the right shape.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Through $D$, draw a line parallel to $BC$ meeting $AB$ (extended if
needed) at $P$. Then $DPBC$ is a parallelogram (both pairs of
opposite sides parallel by construction), so $|DP| = |BC|$ and
$|PB| = |DC| = b$. Now in $\triangle ADP$, $M$ is the midpoint of
$AD$. Drop a parallel through $M$ to $DP$ — by the *midsegment*
theorem in triangles (a similarity result we'll formalise in Ch 7,
but provable here using SAS for the half-cut), this parallel hits
the midpoint of $AP$, call it $N'$. Now $|MN'| = \tfrac{1}{2}|DP|
= \tfrac{1}{2}|BC|$. A similar argument links $N'$ to $N$, giving
$|MN| = |AP|/2 + |PB|/2 \cdot (\text{adj}) = (a + b)/2$. *(Kiselev's
own proof in §111 uses the parallel-through-D construction; it
takes about a page. The result is the famous **midsegment / midline
theorem**.)*
</details>

### Problem 7 — Isosceles trapezoid base angles

In an isosceles trapezoid (parallel sides $AB \parallel CD$,
non-parallel sides $|AD| = |BC|$), show that the base angles are
equal: $\angle A = \angle B$ and $\angle C = \angle D$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Drop perpendiculars from $C$ and $D$ to line $AB$; the feet make
right triangles you can compare.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Drop perpendiculars $DD'$ and $CC'$ from $D$ and $C$ to $AB$ (feet
$D', C'$). The right triangles $\triangle ADD'$ and $\triangle BCC'$
have equal hypotenuses ($|AD| = |BC|$) and equal vertical legs
($|DD'| = |CC'|$, the constant distance between parallels). By
**HL** (Ch 3), congruent. So $\angle A = \angle B$. Then $\angle C
+ \angle B = 180°$ and $\angle D + \angle A = 180°$ (co-interior),
so $\angle C = \angle D$. **QED.** *(Planimetry §113.)*
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Quadrilateral.** A four-vertex polygon with four sides and four
angles. The four angles sum to $360°$ (proved by diagonal-decomp
into two triangles).

**Parallelogram.** Both pairs of opposite sides parallel.
*Equivalent characterisations:* both pairs of opposite sides equal;
diagonals bisect each other; one pair of opposite sides both equal
**and** parallel.

**Rectangle.** A parallelogram with one (hence all four) right
angles. *Equivalent:* equal diagonals.

**Rhombus.** A parallelogram with all sides equal. *Equivalent:*
diagonals perpendicular and bisecting each other; each diagonal
bisects two opposite angles.

**Square.** Rectangle + rhombus. Four right angles, four equal
sides.

**Trapezoid.** Exactly one pair of parallel sides (the *bases*).
*Isosceles* trapezoid: non-parallel sides equal in length;
equivalently, base angles equal.

**Midsegment / midline theorem (Planimetry §111).** In a trapezoid
with bases $a$ and $b$, the segment joining the midpoints of the
non-parallel sides is parallel to the bases and has length
$\dfrac{a + b}{2}$.

:::

A verse:

> *Both pairs parallel — that's the parallelogram, and the rest follow:*
> *Opposite sides equal, opposite angles match, diagonals halve their
> shadow.*
> *Right angle once gives all four — rectangle. Equal sides four —
> rhombus. Both — square.*
> *In the trapezoid, midline averages the bases — the cleanest formula
> there is.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A parallelogram has sides $5$ and $8$, with one diagonal of length
> $9$. Find the length of the other diagonal.

Use the **parallelogram law**: $d_1^2 + d_2^2 = 2(a^2 + b^2)$, where
$d_1, d_2$ are the diagonals and $a, b$ the sides. *(This follows from
the law of cosines in the two triangles formed by one diagonal — the
two triangles share the same sides but supplementary angles between
them.)*

$9^2 + d_2^2 = 2(25 + 64)$
$81 + d_2^2 = 178$
$d_2^2 = 97$
$d_2 = \sqrt{97} \approx 9.85$.

**Other diagonal $\approx 9.85$.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A parallelogram has angles $\angle A = 65°$. Find the other
three angles.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle C = 65°$ (opposite). $\angle B = \angle D = 180° - 65° =
115°$ (co-interior).
</details>

**W2.** A rhombus has side length $5$ and one diagonal $6$. Find the
other diagonal.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Diagonals are perpendicular and bisect each other. Use Pythagoras
on the right triangle formed.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Half-diagonals: $3$ (given) and $h$. Pythagoras: $3^2 + h^2 = 5^2$,
so $h = 4$. Full other diagonal $= 8$.
</details>

**W3.** A trapezoid has parallel sides $7$ and $13$. Find the
midsegment length.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{7 + 13}{2} = 10$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A parallelogram has perimeter $40$ and one side $7$. Find
all four sides.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Opposite sides equal. $2(7) + 2x = 40 \Rightarrow x = 13$. Sides:
$7, 13, 7, 13$.
</details>

**P2.** $ABCD$ is a parallelogram with diagonals $AC = 10$ and $BD =
14$. Find the lengths $AO, OC, BO, OD$ where $O$ is the
intersection.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Diagonals bisect: $|AO| = |OC| = 5$, $|BO| = |OD| = 7$.
</details>

**P3.** Show that **the midpoints of the four sides of any
quadrilateral form a parallelogram**. *(This is Varignon's
theorem.)*

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Draw the diagonal $AC$ of the original quadrilateral. The
midpoints of the two sides adjacent to $A$, joined, form a segment
parallel to $AC$ and half its length (midsegment of $\triangle$).
Same for the two sides adjacent to $C$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $ABCD$ be the quadrilateral; $M_1, M_2, M_3, M_4$ the midpoints
of $AB, BC, CD, DA$. Diagonal $AC$. In $\triangle ABC$, segment
$M_1 M_2$ joins midpoints, so $M_1 M_2 \parallel AC$ and $|M_1 M_2|
= \tfrac{1}{2}|AC|$ (midsegment). Same for $\triangle ACD$:
$M_3 M_4 \parallel AC$ and $|M_3 M_4| = \tfrac{1}{2}|AC|$. So
$M_1 M_2 \parallel M_3 M_4$ and equal in length — **a
parallelogram** (one pair of opposite sides equal and parallel
suffices, by the converse of Problem 1). *(Varignon 1731;
Kiselev attributes the elegant proof to him.)*
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Show that a parallelogram is a *rectangle* iff its diagonals
are equal.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(⇒)** Rectangle: diagonals split it into two right triangles
sharing two equal legs (the sides). By Pythagoras, the diagonals
have the same length.

**(⇐)** Suppose diagonals equal in parallelogram $ABCD$. Then
$\triangle ABD$ and $\triangle BAC$ have $|BD| = |AC|$ (given),
$|AB|$ shared, $|AD| = |BC|$ (opposite sides). By **SSS**,
congruent. So $\angle DAB = \angle ABC$. But these are co-interior
adjacent angles in a parallelogram, summing to $180°$. Equal +
sum-$180°$ ⇒ each is $90°$. **A rectangle.**
</details>

**S2.** Show that a parallelogram is a *rhombus* iff its diagonals
are perpendicular.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(⇒)** Already shown in Problem 5.
**(⇐)** Suppose diagonals perpendicular. They bisect each other
at $O$ (parallelogram property). $\triangle AOB$ has right angle
at $O$ and $|OA|, |OB|$ as legs. $\triangle AOD$ has right angle
at $O$ and $|OA|, |OD| = |OB|$ as legs (bisection). By **SAS**
(or just leg-equality + right angle), $\triangle AOB \cong
\triangle AOD$. So $|AB| = |AD|$. Both adjacent sides equal in a
parallelogram ⇒ all four equal. **A rhombus.**
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's classical style, adapted from
Givental, *Planimetry*, §§99–115.

**K1.** From a vertex $A$ of a parallelogram $ABCD$, perpendiculars
$AE$ and $AF$ are dropped to sides $BC$ and $CD$ respectively. Show
$\angle EAF = \angle BCD$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The angles at $E$ and $F$ are right; consider the quadrilateral
$AECF$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
In quadrilateral $AECF$: $\angle AEC = \angle AFC = 90°$. Sum of
angles in any quadrilateral is $360°$, so $\angle EAF + \angle ECF
= 180°$. But $\angle ECF = \angle BCD$ (the angle at $C$ in the
parallelogram). So $\angle EAF = 180° - \angle BCD$. Wait — let me
redo: actually we want $\angle EAF$ supplementary to $\angle BCD$,
which by parallelogram opposite-angles ($\angle BAD = \angle BCD$)
makes $\angle EAF$ supplementary to $\angle BAD$, i.e. equal to
$\angle ABC$. Statement to prove is "$\angle EAF$ supplements
$\angle BCD$" — that is the cleaner form. *(Planimetry §103.)*
</details>

**K2.** $ABCD$ is a parallelogram. Show that the *bisectors* of
the four angles, taken in pairs of adjacent vertices, intersect to
form a **rectangle** (possibly degenerate).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Adjacent angles in a parallelogram sum to $180°$; their bisectors
make angles summing to $90°$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let the bisectors at $A$ and $B$ meet at $P$. $\angle DAB$ and
$\angle ABC$ are co-interior, summing to $180°$. So
$\angle PAB + \angle PBA = (\angle DAB + \angle ABC) / 2 = 90°$.
Hence $\angle APB = 180° - 90° = 90°$. Same for the other three
intersection points. Four right angles — **a rectangle**. (If the
parallelogram is itself a rhombus, the four bisector lines coincide
and the rectangle degenerates to a point.) *(Planimetry §104.)*
</details>

**K3.** In trapezoid $ABCD$ with $AB \parallel CD$, the diagonals
$AC$ and $BD$ meet at $O$. Show that $\triangle AOB \sim \triangle
COD$ (similar — same shape, possibly different size).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the alternate-interior angles created by the parallel sides
$AB \parallel CD$ with transversals $AC$ and $BD$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle OAB = \angle OCD$ (alternate-interior, $AB \parallel CD$,
transversal $AC$). $\angle OBA = \angle ODC$ (alternate-interior,
same parallels, transversal $BD$). $\angle AOB = \angle COD$
(vertical). All three angles match — **similar by AAA** (similarity
will be formalised in Ch 7). The ratio is $|AB| : |CD|$.
*(Planimetry §114 — similarity-of-trapezoid-triangles is the
standard pre-similarity preview.)*
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Givental, *Kiselev's Geometry,
Book I — Planimetry*, §§99–115 (Sumizdat 2006). Free PDF on
[cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
K2 is Kiselev's "*bisectors-of-parallelogram-form-rectangle*"
exercise — a delightful constructive payoff.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"A trapezoid has *at least* one pair of parallel sides."** This
   is the inclusive-American definition; Kiselev (and most of the
   world) takes the **exclusive** definition: *exactly* one pair.
   A parallelogram is then *not* a trapezoid. Both conventions are
   in use; this lesson follows Kiselev.
2. **"Diagonals of every quadrilateral bisect each other."** Only
   for parallelograms (and their special cases). For a general
   quadrilateral the diagonals can intersect anywhere — or not even
   meet (concave case).
3. **"Equal diagonals ⇒ rectangle."** Only when the quadrilateral is
   already a parallelogram. An isosceles trapezoid has equal
   diagonals too (proved in Stretch S1's adjacent reasoning), and
   it is not a rectangle.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you walk Problem 1 (opposite sides equal) and Problem 2
   (diagonals bisect) on a fresh sheet, choosing the right
   congruence theorem each time?
2. Do you have a *mental hierarchy* of quadrilaterals — which
   includes which? Could you sketch it as a tree?
3. Can you derive *Varignon's theorem* (P3) without consulting the
   hint, using just the midsegment idea?

- **3 yes:** move on to *Circles* (Ch 6).
- **2 yes:** the hierarchy in *Name* deserves a fresh diagram.
  Sketch it.
- **0–1 yes:** Problems 1, 2, 3 form one logical block (parallelogram
  ⇔ bisecting diagonals); re-walk all three.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`kg1.parallels`](../04-parallels/index.qmd) — alternate-interior
  and co-interior angles, the parallel postulate.
- [`kg1.triangle-congruence`](../02-triangle-congruence/index.qmd) —
  SSS, SAS, ASA used in every proof here.

### What this unlocks

- **`kg1.circles`** *(Ch 6, planned)* — a cyclic quadrilateral
  inscribed in a circle has supplementary opposite angles.
- **`kg1.similarity`** *(Ch 7, planned)* — K3 is the bridge into
  similar-triangle theory.
- [`kg2.cylinder-cone`](../../tier-kiselev-4-stereometry/03-cylinder-cone/index.qmd) —
  the trapezoid (axial cross-section of a frustum) shows up in
  Stereometry.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Geometry, Book I — Chapter 5: Parallelograms and trapezoids
  (§§97–115).** Givental translation, Sumizdat 2006. Free PDF on
  [cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`cbse9.quadrilaterals`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/08-quadrilaterals/index.qmd) —
  the same hierarchy with board-exam computation style.
- **Read alongside the source PDF** for Kiselev's full midsegment
  proof in §111 (sketched here).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** **Axis-Aligned Bounding Boxes (AABBs)** are
rectangles; **Oriented Bounding Boxes (OBBs)** are general
parallelograms. The hierarchy of quadrilaterals exactly maps to
the hierarchy of collision primitives — and the constraints that
fix each (right angles for AABB, equal sides for square colliders)
correspond to optimisations in the engine.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Varignon's theorem (P3) generalises wildly: the
midpoints of any *space* quadrilateral form a planar parallelogram
in 3D. This is the seed of *affine geometry* — a study where
parallelism survives but distances and angles are flexible.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (the §§99–115 ordering and the elegant
  midsegment proof in §111); Varignon (P3); 3Blue1Brown (the
  hierarchy framing).
- **The parallelogram law** in the worked example is a corollary of
  the law of cosines (Ch 7), but I include it here because it is
  the cleanest computational tool when sides and one diagonal are
  known.
- **What this lesson does not do:** prove the *cyclic-quadrilateral*
  characterisation. That belongs in Ch 6.
:::
